---
title: "Joining 130 million points"
author:
  - name: Ralph Straumann
    url: https://ralphstraumann.ch
date: "2026-05-07 21:01"
image: "zones.png"
description: "Dewey Dunnington revisits his 2024 benchmark of #spatialjoin operations on 130 million points, now testing #SedonaDB and #DuckDB #Spatial in comparison to the original tools. The results are remarkable: 6 seconds on a laptop replacing 3-5 minutes and 50 GB of RAM, a 30x-50x speedup achieved in just over a year."
---

Back in December 2024, [Dewey Dunnington (spatially) joined 130,000,000 building 
centroid points][post1] from the Microsoft U.S. buildings dataset to 33,000 
postal code areas. The idea was to benchmark various tools: QGIS, PostGIS, 
GeoPandas[^geopandas] (using two different approaches), [DuckDB Geography][duckdbgeography][^duckdb], 
and [Apache Sedona][sedona]. That [2024 article][post1] is interesting in 
itself.

Fast forward to the present day and a [new article][post2]: Dewey now works at 
[Wherobots][wherobots] and has been building [SedonaDB][sedonadb][^sedonadb] - which is "a 
tool purpose-built to make exactly that sort of wrangling and joining 
effortless" (Dewey's characterisation) (and which is different from [Apache 
Sedona][apachesedona]!). In the [current article][post2], Dewey re-ran the 2024 
analysis but using new approaches: SedonaDB and DuckDB Spatial (which is 
different from the afore-mentioned DuckDB Geography!):

> I checked the summary on my initial post, and it looks like the fastest 
version I was able to come up with was somewhere around 3-5 minutes with quite 
a bit of explicit wrangling/tuning and 50 GB of memory involved.
> 
> If you have the files locally (as I did in the original post), SedonaDB 0.3.0 
executes the join in 6 seconds on my laptop (Apple M4, 24G RAM) without any 
tuning or wrangling.

For DuckDB Spatial, "the `ST_Contains()` version of the query takes ~1m 30s; 
however, the `ST_DWithin(..., 0)` approximation is much faster (13 seconds)."

So a 30x to 50x speed-up within a bit more than a year. If you (have to) care 
about fast and efficient data pipelines, I've got to agree with Dewey's 
conclusion: "It’s an exciting time to be working in spatial!"


[^geopandas]: [`GeoPandas`](https://geopandas.org/en/stable/getting_started/introduction.html) is an extension to the popular data science library `Pandas` that enables support for geospatial data.
[^duckdb]: [`DuckDB`](https://www.duckdb.org/why_duckdb) is an open-source column-oriented Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) designed for analytics use-cases.
[^sedonadb]: [`SedonaDB`](https://sedona.apache.org/sedonadb/latest/) is an open-source single-node analytical database engine with geospatial as a first-class citizen.

[post1]: https://dewey.dunnington.ca/post/2024/wrangling-and-joining-130m-points-with-duckdb--the-open-source-spatial-stack/
[post2]: https://dewey.dunnington.ca/post/2026/wrangling-and-joining-130m-points...in-2026/
[duckdbgeography]: https://github.com/paleolimbot/duckdb-geography
[sedona]: https://dewey.dunnington.ca/post/2024/apache...sedona-you-say/
[wherobots]: https://wherobots.com/
[sedonadb]: https://sedona.apache.org/sedonadb/latest/
[apachesedona]: https://sedona.apache.org/latest/
